items.q: What is the difference between alpha bias and beta bias?
items.correct: Alpha bias exaggerates differences between genders; beta bias minimises or ignores them.
items.misconception: They both mean treating men and women exactly the same.
items.random_wrong: Alpha bias is about age and beta bias about culture.
items.why: Students confuse the two forms of gender bias, mixing up exaggeration with minimisation.

items.q: Does determinism mean behaviour can never be predicted or changed?
items.correct: No; determinism says behaviour has causes, which actually allows it to be predicted and influenced.
items.misconception: Determinism means behaviour is random and unknowable.
items.why: Learners wrongly equate determinism with randomness rather than lawful causation.

items.q: Is reductionism always a weakness in psychology?
items.correct: No; reducing behaviour to simpler parts can give precise, testable explanations, though it may oversimplify.
items.misconception: Reductionism is always bad and never useful.
items.random_wrong: Reductionism means studying only large groups.
items.why: Students treat reductionism as purely negative, missing its scientific advantages.

items.q: Exaggerating the differences between men and women is an example of:
items.why: Correct answer: Alpha bias. “Beta bias” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Judging other cultures by the standards of one's own is known as:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ethnocentrism. “Reductionism” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The view that all behaviour is caused by factors beyond our control is called:
items.why: It's Determinism. “Free will” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Studying a single individual in rich, detailed depth is characteristic of the:
items.why: Correct answer: Idiographic approach. “Nomothetic approach” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
